comments.text: I did an intensive back-and-forth with GPT-5.4 Pro on this problem and here is the write-up with: a) an unconditional partial result $ r(n):=\#\{(a,b)\in A^2:\ a+b=n\} \ll_\epsilon n^{2/5+\epsilon}$ b) the strongest exact reformulations we currently have for a coprime model, and isolate where the square-sieve approach really breaks. c) summarization of the 2025–2026 literature surrounding squarefull values, modular square roots, quadratic large sieves, and related averaging problems, and explain precisely which theorem is still missing. There is an on-going research work, so noting this will probably help finish the full problem in the near future (either by humans or new LLMs).
Thanks. Standard check finds no issues.

There's an interesting geometric approach to the problem and a pretty extensive literature focused on that if you search for Campana points, Manin-type conjectures. Start with the identity \(r(n)=(1_A*1_A)(n)=\#\{(a,b)\in A^2:a+b=n\}\). View \((a,b)\) as an integral point on the affine plane \(\mathbb A^2\subset \mathbb P^2\) via \((a,b)\leftrightarrow [x:y:z]=[a:b:1]\), and impose the linear condition \(a+b=n\) as the moving line\[ L_n:\ x+y=nz\subset \mathbb P^2. \]Now encode “powerful” as an orbifold integrality condition (Campana condition) along the coordinate divisors \(D_x=\{x=0\}\), \(D_y=\{y=0\}\) by taking the boundary\[ \Delta=\Big(1-\frac12\Big)(D_x+D_y). \]Then a rational point \([x:y:1]\) is a \(\Delta\)-Campana \(\mathbb Z\)-point precisely when for every prime \(p\), the valuation \(v_p(x)\) (resp. \(v_p(y)\)) is either \(0\) or \(\ge 2\), i.e. \(x\) and \(y\) are powerful. The pair \((\mathbb P^2,\Delta)\) is klt and log Fano because \(-\!\big(K_{\mathbb P^2}+\Delta\big)\) is ample. In this language, the question is a uniform fiber problem: bound the number of Campana \(\mathbb Z\)-points on the members \(L_n\), i.e.\[ r(n)=\#\big(L_n(\mathbb Q)\cap (\mathbb P^2,\Delta)(\mathbb Z)\big)\stackrel{?}{\ll_\varepsilon} n^\varepsilon \quad(\forall \varepsilon>0). \]The paper arXiv:2104.14946 analyzes closely related phenomena for Campana points on \(\mathbb P^1\) with boundary \(D=\tfrac12[0]+\tfrac12[1]+\tfrac12[\infty]\), which (on an integral model) forces squareful/squarefull-type divisibility conditions on the homogeneous coordinates and certain linear combinations; in particular it highlights that, even in very low-dimensional log Fano orbifolds, the expected Manin-type leading constant can be destabilized by Campana thin subsets and “accumulating” contributions coming from covers or special subfamilies. A key takeaway is that “log Fano + Campana-Manin” by itself does not automatically give uniform control on individual fibers of a fibration (or pencil of curves): thin sets can be engineered so that removing them changes leading constants dramatically, and there may be no canonical geometrically-defined thin set whose removal yields the “correct” constant. For our \(r(n)\), this is a warning that any approach that counts Campana points globally on \((\mathbb P^2,\Delta)\) and then hopes to deduce pointwise bounds on intersections with \(L_n\) must also control how exceptional thin sets intersect the pencil \(\{L_n\}\) uniformly in \(n\). Beyond 2104.14946, a precise route to \(r(n)=n^{o(1)}\) is to strengthen the log-Fano/Campana counting philosophy from global asymptotics to uniform fiberwise non-concentration. One clean conjectural package is: (i) a Campana–Manin asymptotic for \((\mathbb P^2,\Delta)\) after removing a thin exceptional set \(T\), and simultaneously (ii) a uniform bound \(\#(T\cap L_n)\ll_\varepsilon n^\varepsilon\) for all \(n\), so that thin exceptional points cannot “pile up” on a single line \(L_n\). This is naturally tied to Hilbert-property/weak-approximation expectations for Campana points (which aim to prevent the Campana set itself from being thin and to constrain thin images), combined with an effective uniformity principle for the pencil (morally: the Campana points equidistribute across the base \(\mathbb P^1\) at subpolynomial scale). If one could prove (or assume) such a fiberwise thin-set control statement for this specific log Fano orbifold, then the desired bound \(r(n)\ll_\varepsilon n^\varepsilon\) would follow immediately from the geometric reformulation.
It may be helpful to clarify that for this problem, * refers to additive convolution rather than Dirichlet convolution.

Here $1_A\ast 1_A(n)$ denotes the additive convolution, not multiplicative, so it is counting the number of $a,b\in A$ such that $a+b=n$. (In particular $1_A\ast 1_A(p)=0$ for all prime $p$ is false.) Even if it were true, it does not disprove $1_A\ast 1_A(n)=n^{o(1)}$, since that is (in a slight abuse of notation) short for saying, for all $\epsilon>0$, if $n$ is sufficiently large then\[1_A\ast 1_A(n)\leq n^\epsilon.\]
